docs.title: Setting Up Heros
docs.text:
Heros are simple HTML elements styled by the project's stylesheet. Ensure your mkdocs.yml includes the Material theme and your custom CSS where hero styles are defined (typically in style.css):

# mkdocs.yml
theme:
  name: material
  custom_dir: wiki/wiki_design

extra_css:
  - styles/style.css

If you need custom hero colours or sizes, add variants in styles/style.css following the existing naming convention (e.g., .hero--indigo, .hero--lg).

References
  • Material for MkDocs — Customisation guide

docs.title: Icons & Emojis
docs.text: Icons and emojis help convey meaning quickly — use them sparingly and consistently.

docs.title: Design Standards
docs.text:
Dos

  • Do use icons to reinforce meaning, not replace clear text.
  • Do prefer the theme’s icon shortcodes (Material, Octicons, Font Awesome) for consistency.
  • Do include accessible text when an icon conveys important information.

Don'ts

  • Don’t overuse emojis in formal documentation.
  • Don’t rely on icons alone for semantic meaning; include text labels.

docs.title: Using Icons & Emojis
docs.text:
Material supports built-in icon shortcodes and emoji. Examples:

Inline icon shortcode (Material icons):

This is important 

This is important :material-check:

Font Awesome example (if enabled):

Send 

Send :fontawesome-solid-paper-plane:

Emoji usage:

We shipped the feature 🎉

We shipped the feature 🎉

To use an external icon set like Font Awesome, enable the CSS in mkdocs.yml and use the matching shortcode or HTML.

docs.title: Setting Up Icons & Emojis
docs.text:
Material ships with multiple icon sets. To override or add icons in mkdocs.yml:

theme:
  icon:
    logo: material/rocket
    annotation: material/arrow-right-circle

To enable Font Awesome shortcodes, add the stylesheet via extra_css (or host locally):

extra_css:
  - https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.4.0/css/all.min.css

Notes: - Shortcodes map to CSS class names under the hood; refer to the Material reference for the exact shortcode names.

References
  • Material for MkDocs — Icons & Emojis reference

docs.title: Images
docs.text: Images illustrate concepts and should be used thoughtfully to support text.

docs.title: Image Design Standards
docs.text:
Dos

  • Do use descriptive alt text for all images.
  • Do optimise images for web (compress, use appropriate formats like WebP when possible).
  • Do provide captions or surrounding text that explains the image's purpose.

Don'ts

  • Don’t rely on images as the only way to convey information.
  • Don’t include overly large images that slow page loads.

docs.title: Using images
docs.text:
Markdown image syntax:

![Diagram showing data flow](assets/images/data-flow.png)

For responsive images, consider serving multiple sizes and using HTML picture/srcset where needed. Material supports local assets and custom docs_overrides folders for images.

To add captions, put the image in a figure with a caption in HTML or use surrounding text.

docs.title: Setting Up Images
docs.text:
No special Markdown extensions are required for basic images. For responsive or advanced workflows you can:

  • Use an image processing pipeline (build step) to generate sizes/formats.
  • Use extra_css to set max-width and responsive behavior, for example:
.md-typeset img {
  max-width: 100%;
  height: auto;
}

Notes: - Store images under wiki/wiki_design/assets/images (or your chosen docs assets directory) and reference paths relative to pages.

References
  • Material for MkDocs — Images reference

docs.title: Lists
docs.text: Lists improve scanability and organise related items. Choose the right list type for the content.

docs.title: List Design Standards
docs.text:
Dos

  • Do use unordered lists for collections of related items and ordered lists for steps or rank-ordered items.
  • Do keep list items short and parallel in structure.

Don'ts

  • Don’t nest lists deeply — flatten or break into sub-sections when needed.
  • Don’t use lists for long paragraphs; use paragraphs or tables instead.

docs.title: Using Lists
docs.text:
Unordered list:

  • Item one
  • Item two
  • Subitem
- Item one
- Item two
  - Subitem

Ordered list:

  1. Step one
  2. Step two
1. Step one
2. Step two

Definition list (Markdown extension def_list or HTML):

Term Definition for the term. 
Term
: Definition for the term.

Attribute lists (attr_list) can be used to add classes or IDs to lists for styling or behaviour.

docs.title: Setting Up Admonitions
docs.text: Most lists work without extra configuration. Consider enabling attr_list and def_list if you rely on list attributes or definition-style lists:

docs.title:
markdown_extensions:
  - attr_list
  - def_list
docs.text:
References
  • Material for MkDocs — Lists reference

docs.title: Page References
docs.text: Material for MkDocs uses page-level metadata to control how pages appear in navigation, search results, social cards, and custom theme layouts.

docs.title: Design Standards
docs.text:
Dos

  • Do set a clear title for every page so navigation, search, and sharing are consistent.
  • Do use subtitle to add context to a page when it helps readers understand the content at a glance.
  • Do use status only when the page state is meaningful, such as new, deprecated, or updated.

Don'ts

  • Don’t rely on the generated filename or heading alone for page metadata; explicit front matter is more reliable.
  • Don’t overuse icons or statuses; use them sparingly so the sidebar remains readable.
  • Don’t put important metadata only in the file name or directory structure; keep page metadata in front matter when you need consistent presentation.

docs.title: Page front matter
docs.text: Material for MkDocs supports standard page metadata in YAML front matter. Add metadata at the top of the Markdown file, inside --- markers.

docs.title: title
docs.text:
The page title is used by the navigation sidebar, the page header, and search results.

  • MkDocs may infer a title from the first heading or filename if title is omitted.
  • Explicit title values are more stable across theme changes and navigation structures.
---
title: Page References
---

docs.title: subtitle
docs.text:
A subtitle appears below the title in the sidebar, helping readers understand the page purpose.

---
subtitle: Front matter and metadata for Material pages
---

docs.title: description
docs.text:
The description is used for meta tags and social cards. It can also improve search previews.

---
description: Add page metadata for navigation, search, and custom theme rendering.
---

docs.title: icon
docs.text:
Use icon to assign a Material icon to the page in the navigation sidebar.

---
icon: material/emoticon-happy
---
  • Icons should use the material/ prefix and a valid Material icon name.
  • Choose icons that visually match the page purpose without being distracting.

docs.title: status
docs.text:
Statuses are defined in mkdocs.yml and then referenced in page front matter.

extra:
  status:
    new: Recently added
    deprecated: Will be removed soon
    beta: Experimental content

---
status: new
---
  • Status badges can be rendered in the navigation sidebar and page lists.
  • Use meaningful status names that align with the documentation workflow.

docs.title: template
docs.text:
If you are using a custom theme override, template lets a page use a different Jinja template.

---
template: custom.html
---
  • template is only valid when the theme provides a matching template file.

docs.title: Best practices for page metadata
docs.text:
• Keep metadata concise and reader-focused.
  • Use title and description to support search and social sharing.
  • Avoid repeating the same copy in both the page title and subtitle.

docs.title: Example page metadata
docs.text:
---
title: Page References
subtitle: Configure page metadata for Material for MkDocs
description: Reference the front matter values supported by Material for MkDocs and how they affect navigation, search, and theming.
icon: material/book-open
status: new
---
References
  • Material for MkDocs — References
  • Material for MkDocs — Configuration reference

docs.title: Tooltips
docs.text: Tooltips provide compact, on-hover contextual help for links, icons and other inline elements. Material for MkDocs can replace the native browser title rendering with a consistent, theme-aware tooltip when the feature is enabled.

docs.title: Tooltips Design standards
docs.text:
Dos

  • Do use tooltips for short clarifications (1–2 lines) such as definitions, short hints, or link previews.
  • Do prefer inline tooltip content that supplements rather than repeats the main text.
  • Do provide clear, plain-language tooltip — avoid long sentences or complex HTML.

Don'ts

  • Don’t put critical instructions or essential information inside tooltips — some users cannot access hover interactions.
  • Don’t overload tooltips with links or large blocks of content.
  • Don’t rely on tooltips for accessibility-only content; ensure the same meaning is available in the document flow.

docs.title: Basic Tooltips
docs.text:
Attribute lists can be used to add a title to icons or other inline elements:

:material-information-outline:{ title="Important information" }

docs.title: Link tooltips (inline)
docs.text:
Linked tooltips allow the addition of a title for each link that appears as a tooltip on-hover

Hover over me

[Hover over me](https://example.com "I'm a tooltip link!")

docs.title: Link tooltips (reference style)
docs.text:
You can also add tooltips using reference-style links:

Hover over me too

[Hover over me too][example]

[example]: https://example.com "I'm a tooltip link!"

docs.title: Abbreviations
docs.text:
Defining abbreviations will automatically show tooltip-like expansions for acronyms. Abbreviations can be defined by starting with a * and immediately followed by the term or acronym to be associated in square brackets:

The HTML specification is maintained by the W3C.

The HTML specification is maintained by the W3C.

*[HTML]: Hyper Text Markup Language
*[W3C]: World Wide Web Consortium

Adding a glossary

The Snippets extension can be used to implement a simple glossary by moving all abbreviations in a dedicated file, and auto-append this file to all pages with the following configuration:

abbreviations.mdmkdoc.yml 
    *[HTML]: Hyper Text Markup Language
    *[W3C]: World Wide Web Consortium

    markdown_extensions:
        - pymdownx.snippets:
            auto_append:
                - glossary.md

docs.title: Setting up tooltips
docs.text:
Enable the improved tooltips feature in mkdocs.yml and add required Markdown extensions for abbreviations and attribute lists:

# mkdocs.yml
theme:
  features:
    - content.tooltips

markdown_extensions:
  - abbr
  - attr_list
  - pymdownx.snippets
  • content.tooltips: replaces native title rendering with theme-styled tooltips.
  • abbr: supports abbreviation definitions that expand into inline tooltip-like content.
  • attr_list: allows adding title attributes on arbitrary elements (icons, spans, etc.).
  • pymdownx.snippets: optional — useful to centralise glossary/abbreviation files and auto-append them to pages.

Note: improved tooltips require Material for MkDocs v9.5.0 or later.

References
  • Material for MkDocs — Tooltips reference
  • Python Markdown — Attribute Lists
